title: Discovery of hidden order in the Shastry-Sutherland magnet Nd2Be2GeO7
authors: Andi Liu, Samuel H. Moody, Thomas J. Hicken, Jonas A. Krieger, Hubertus Luetkens, George D. A. Wood, Helen C. Walker, Zhendong Fu, Jason S. Gardner, Jinkui Zhao, Zhaoming Tian, Hanjie Guo
abstract: Hidden order typically manifests as a thermodynamic phase transition without a conventional order parameter, leaving its true nature concealed even at the lowest temperatures. In the frustrated Shastry-Sutherland magnet Nd$_2$Be$_2$GeO$_7$, we observe a related yet fundamentally distinct phenomenon. A sharp specific-heat anomaly appears at 250 mK, but zero-field neutron diffraction and muon spin relaxation detect no static magnetism down to 100 and 30 mK, respectively, pointing to a hidden-order state. Remarkably, this hidden order does not emerge under an applied magnetic field, but instead reveals itself only after the field is applied and subsequently removed where magnetic Bragg peaks appear, albeit with strongly suppressed moments. A glassy state is ruled out by ac susceptibility and specific heat measurements. Complementary $μ$SR measurements reveal coherent spin fluctuations at a rate on the order of gigahertz. Taken together, these results suggest that the system lies in close proximity to the quantum spin liquid and long-range magnetic order state such that a small perturbation can effectively drive the system towards distinct ground states. These findings also distinguish Nd$_2$Be$_2$GeO$_7$ from known frustrated systems, establishing it as a unique platform where the synergistic interplay among the spin-orbit coupling, crystal field, and magnetic frustration leads to unexpected quantum states.
summary: This work investigates the hidden-order state in the frustrated magnet Nd2Be2GeO7 using multiple advanced techniques like muSR and neutron diffraction. The key finding is that magnetic order appears only after field cycling, suggesting a unique ground state governed by spin-orbit coupling and frustration. This establishes the material as a distinct platform for studying quantum phase transitions.
main_problem: The study investigates a 'hidden order' state in the frustrated Shastry-Sutherland magnet Nd2Be2GeO7, characterized by thermodynamic anomalies without conventional magnetic ordering signatures.
main_result: The system exhibits a hidden-order phase whose magnetic signature only emerges after cycling an applied magnetic field, suggesting proximity to quantum spin liquid or long-range ordered states.
method: A combination of techniques was used, including specific heat measurements, zero-field and longitudinal-field muon spin relaxation (muSR), neutron diffraction, and Electron Spin Resonance (ESR).
model_or_system: The physical system is the Shastry-Sutherland magnet Nd2Be2GeO7. The magnetic behavior is modeled by considering the interplay of spin-orbit coupling, crystal field effects, and magnetic frustration.
key_observables: Sharp specific heat anomaly at 250 mK; absence of static magnetism in zero-field muSR/neutron diffraction; appearance of magnetic Bragg peaks only after field cycling; coherent spin fluctuations on the order of gigahertz.
important_parameters:
The excitation gap ($\Delta$ or $\delta$) is consistently found around $8.5 - 8.7$ meV; fluctuation rates ($
u$) are in the GHz range.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the observed phenomena can be explained by a combination of CEF effects, magnetic frustration, and proximity to quantum critical points.
figures_summary: Figures show CEF excitations via INS, evolution of neutron diffraction intensities under field cycling (Fig. 2b), and temperature dependence of susceptibility/magnetization.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by establishing the hidden-order nature through multiple measurements (specific heat, muSR, NPD). It then uses detailed analysis of CEF excitations and field response to distinguish this state from known frustrated magnets, culminating in a discussion of its unique quantum ground state.
why_interesting: The history-dependent emergence of magnetic order upon field cycling is a hallmark of complex quantum phase transitions, providing experimental evidence for exotic correlated states near quantum criticality.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Crystal electric ﬁeld excitations. a-b, Inelastic neutron scattering intensity maps collected with incident neutron energies Ei of 53.1 and 25.1 meV, respectively, at 5 K. The red arrows indicate the CEF excitations. c, Momentum transfer (|Q|) dependence of the integrated intensities (I ) for the excitations at around 9 and 37 meV. The red curves are ﬁts according to the form factor of Nd3+, I = I0f 2(Q) + Ibg, where I0 and Ibg are ﬁtting parameters. d, Energy (E) dependence of the intensities integrated over |Q| along with a CEF ﬁt. The green dashed lines are backgrounds. The error bars in c-d correspond to statistical errors equal to the square root of the intensities. e,...

FIG. 2. Neutron diﬀraction measurements. a, Neutron powder diﬀraction pattern for Nd2Be2GeO7 collected at 2 K, along with a Rietveld reﬁnement based on the crystal structure with the space group P¯421m. The excluded regions correspond to the peaks from the copper holder and the aluminum from the cryostat. b, Evolution of the low-angle intensities under a magnetic ﬁeld cycled from 0 T to 6 T and back to 0 T at 0.1 K. c, Magnetic structure reﬁnement based on the irreducible representation (IR) Γ2 against the diﬀerence pattern (I0.1 K −I2 K). d, Representative reﬁnements based on other IRs. e, Magnetic structure for Nd2Be2GeO7 based on the best reﬁnement according to Γ2. The red and green...

FIG. 3. Zero-ﬁeld µSR measurements. a-b, Representative zero-ﬁeld µSR spectra measured at various temperatures on the GPS and FLAME spectrometers, respectively. The solid curves are ﬁts according to Eq. 1. c-d, Temperature dependence of the fast and slow muon spin relaxation rate λf and λs. e, Temperature dependence of the amplitudes Af and As. The error bars in a-b and c-e correspond to statistical errors and standard errors from the ﬁts, respectively.

FIG. 4. Longitudinal-ﬁeld µSR measurements. a, Representative µSR spectra measured at various external magnetic ﬁelds at 80 mK. The solid curves are ﬁts according to Eq. 1. The inset shows the LF measurements at 120 K. The error bars are statistical errors. b, Temperature dependence of the relaxation rates λf and λs. The error bars are standard errors of the ﬁts. The red curve is a ﬁt to λf as described in the text.
